summary: DeVault argues that software reliability has catastrophically declined as developers have embraced a 'move fast and break things' mentality. He observes that tech-savvy users have built up enormous institutional knowledge just to work around broken software, while non-technical users are left bewildered. The post catalogs everyday failures — from reimplemented HTML forms to broken SMS delivery — as evidence of industry-wide recklessness. He calls on developers to prioritize simplicity, rigorously handle state and errors, use proven technologies, and stop letting economics override empathy for users.
key_points: Technical users have unconsciously accumulated vast workaround knowledge for broken software, creating an invisible barrier for non-technical people
Developers have needlessly reimplemented solved problems (like HTML forms) in JavaScript, breaking accessibility and basic functionality
Simplicity is the foundation of reliability: programs should be simple enough to obviously have no bugs, not complicated enough to have no obvious bugs
State management is the language of robustness — designing data structures that cannot represent invalid states prevents entire classes of bugs
Economics and speed-to-market pressures have displaced empathy, accessibility, and basic quality assurance as development priorities
money_quotes: Almost all software that users encounter in $CURRENTYEAR is straight-up broken, and often badly.
You and I, dear reader, have built up an incredible amount of institutional knowledge about how to deal with broken computers.
Our industry is characterized by institutional recklessness and a callous lack of empathy for our users.
You and I are not smart enough to be clever, so don't try.
There are two kinds of programs: those simple enough to obviously have no bugs, and those complicated enough to have no obvious bugs.
YOU DO NOT NEED A JAVASCRIPT-POWERED CUSTOM TEXTBOX WIDGET.
If you commit and push a line of code that you have not personally watched run and work as expected, you have failed to do your job properly.
We can be profitable and empathetic.
key_insight: The software industry's embrace of complexity and speed over simplicity and testing has produced a landscape of universally broken software that only technical experts can navigate, and developers bear personal responsibility for fixing it.
